summary:
The Royal Cemetery of Ur, in use approximately 2600–2400 BC during the Early Dynastic Period of Mesopotamia, is one of the most remarkable archaeological sites from the ancient world. Excavated by Leonard Woolley between 1922 and 1934, the cemetery contained over 2,000 graves, of which sixteen were identified as 'royal' on the basis of the exceptional wealth of grave goods and evidence of large-scale human sacrifice. These 'death pits' reveal a funerary practice in which retainers — servants, soldiers, musicians, and ladies-in-waiting — accompanied their royal masters into death, apparently voluntarily and with elaborate ceremony.

The grave goods recovered from the Royal Cemetery are among the finest examples of Early Dynastic Sumerian craftsmanship ever found. They include the [[event:1787|Standard of Ur]], a mosaic box depicting scenes of war and feasting; golden helmets and lyres; intricate jewellery of gold, lapis lazuli, and carnelian; and vessels of gold, silver, and alabaster. These objects attest to the extraordinary wealth and international trade connections of Ur at this time — the lapis lazuli came from Afghanistan, the carnelian from the Indus Valley region, and the gold from sources in Anatolia or Arabia.

The individuals buried in the royal tombs are partially identified. The tomb known as PG 800 belonged to someone referred to in later texts as Puabi, a woman of high status whose burial included 74 sacrificed retainers arranged in rows. The identity of those interred in the wealthiest tombs remains debated — they may have been kings, high priests, or both, reflecting the intimate connection between royal and religious authority in Sumerian civilisation. The cemetery provides irreplaceable evidence for the social structure, religious beliefs, material culture, and economic reach of [[event:1720|the Sumerian city-state world]] in its most florid phase.
